questions.answer: D. The function will exhaust the runtime stack or run into an infinite loop when j = 50.
questions.to_find: Which statement about the behavior of function f is TRUE?

understand.plain: The function f has a static variable i initialized to 50. When called with j=50, the condition i==j is true, so it prints 'something' and recursively calls f(i)=f(50) again. Since i is static and always equals 50, calling f(50) again puts the program in the same state, creating infinite recursion that exhausts the runtime stack. For any j not equal to 50, the else branch returns 0 immediately.

keywords.explain: A variable declared static inside a function is initialized only once and retains its value across all calls to the function.
keywords.example: static int i = 50; — i stays 50 no matter how many times f is called.

keywords.explain: When a function calls itself repeatedly and there is no terminating base case that can ever be reached, the call stack grows until the runtime stack is exhausted (stack overflow).
keywords.example: f(50) calls f(50) which calls f(50) — no stopping condition.

YES</text><line x1='110' y1='48' x2='110' y2='70' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#a)'/><rect x='30' y='70' width='160' height='38' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='110' y='94' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>print + call f(50)</text><line x1='110' y1='108' x2='110' y2='130' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#a)'/><rect x='30' y='130' width='160' height='38' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='110' y='154' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>f(50): i==j? YES</text><line x1='110' y1='168' x2='110' y2='190' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#b)'/><text x='110' y='210' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>... stack overflow!</text><rect x='240' y='50' width='160' height='38' class='ok' rx='6'/><text x='320' y='74' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' style='fill:#fff'>f(j): i!=j? else</text><text x='320' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:#fff'>return 0 immediately</text><defs><marker id='a' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='4' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L8,4 L0,8 Z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker><marker id='b' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='4' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L8,4 L0,8 Z' fill='#D85A30'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Call flow diagram: f(50) triggers i==j condition, prints, calls f(50) again — infinite chain leading to stack overflow. f(j!=50) hits else branch and returns 0 immediately.

given.aim: Determine which statement is TRUE about the C function f that has a static local variable i=50.

terms.meaning: Static local variable initialized once to 50; always holds the value 50 throughout the program lifetime.
terms.example: After f returns and is called again, i is still 50.
terms.connects: Ensures i==j is always true when j=50, triggering infinite recursion.

terms.term: k = f(i) recursive call inside if-branch
terms.meaning: When i==j (both are 50), the function calls itself with the same argument 50.
terms.connects: Since i is always 50, this self-call never terminates.

terms.meaning: Memory region used to store function call frames; each function call pushes a new frame, and returning pops it.
terms.example: Each call to f(50) pushes a new frame; with no return, frames accumulate until stack overflows.
terms.connects: Infinite recursion exhausts the finite stack memory.

given.plan: Check each option by tracing f(50) (i==j -> infinite recursion) and f(j!=50) (else branch -> returns 0). Identify which statement is accurate.

steps.title: Trace f(j) when j != 50
steps.apply: When j != 50: i=50 != j, so the else branch executes: return 0. Function returns 0 immediately, no printing. This means option B (prints for ALL j) is FALSE, and option A (returns 0 for ALL j) would require j=50 case to also return 0.
steps.note: For any j != 50, the function works normally and returns 0.

steps.title: Trace f(j) when j = 50
steps.apply:
When j=50: i=50 == j=50, so the if-branch executes:
1. printf("something") is called.
2. k = f(i) = f(50) is called.
3. Inside this recursive call, i=50 and j=50 again — same condition, same behavior.
4. This repeats indefinitely with no base case to stop.
The call stack grows without bound until a stack overflow occurs.
steps.note: The 'return 0' after k=f(i) is never reached because f(50) never returns.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply:
A. FALSE — f(50) does not return 0; it recurses infinitely.
B. FALSE — f(j!=50) goes to else and returns 0 without printing.
C. FALSE — f(50) never reaches its return 0 statement; it loops forever.
D. TRUE — f(50) causes infinite recursion, exhausting the runtime stack (stack overflow).
steps.note: D is the only correct statement.

solution.result: D. The function will exhaust the runtime stack or run into an infinite loop when j = 50.

questions.formula_note: Static variable semantics in C: static locals are initialized once and retain their value across calls. Infinite recursion with no reachable terminating base case causes runtime stack exhaustion (stack overflow).

questions.to_find: The output of the C program (value of c - a - b after function calls).

understand.plain: Two swap functions are defined: f1 swaps by value (copies are swapped inside the function, but the original variables in main remain unchanged), and f2 swaps by pointer (actual values in main are swapped through pointer dereferencing). In main, a=4, b=5, c=6. After f1(a,b), nothing changes. After f2(&b,&c), b and c are actually swapped to b=6 and c=5. The output is c - a - b = 5 - 4 - 6 = -5.

keywords.explain: The function receives copies of the argument values. Any changes made to the parameters inside the function do not affect the original variables in the caller.
keywords.example: f1(a,b) where a=4, b=5: inside f1, local copies are swapped, but main's a and b remain 4 and 5.

keywords.explain: The function receives the memory addresses of the variables. Dereferencing the pointers and modifying the values changes the actual variables in the caller.
keywords.example: f2(&b,&c): *a points to main's b, *b points to main's c; the swap is real and changes b and c in main.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 440 200' width='440' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='415' height='30' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='217' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Initial: a=4, b=5, c=6</text><rect x='10' y='55' width='195' height='65' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='107' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>f1(a, b) — pass by value</text><text x='107' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Only copies are swapped</text><text x='107' y='114' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>main: a=4, b=5, c=6 (no change)</text><rect x='220' y='55' width='210' height='65' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='325' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>f2(&b, &c) — pass by pointer</text><text x='325' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Original values are swapped</text><text x='325' y='114' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>main: b=6, c=5 (actual swap)</text><rect x='10' y='140' width='415' height='45' class='ok' rx='4'/><text x='217' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' style='fill:#fff'>Final: a=4, b=6, c=5</text><text x='217' y='180' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:#fff'>c - a - b = 5 - 4 - 6 = -5</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: State table: initial a=4,b=5,c=6; after f1(a,b) no change (pass-by-value); after f2(&b,&c) b=6,c=5 (actual swap via pointers); result c-a-b = 5-4-6 = -5.

given.aim: Determine the output c - a - b after calling f1 (pass-by-value swap) and f2 (pass-by-pointer swap).

terms.term: a=4, b=5, c=6 (initial values in main)
terms.meaning: Starting values of the three integer variables on main's stack frame.
terms.example: These are local to main and only affected by functions that receive their addresses.
terms.connects: f1 receives copies of a and b (no effect); f2 receives addresses of b and c (actual swap).

terms.meaning: f1 receives copies: its local a and b are swapped but main's original a and b are unchanged.
terms.example: Inside f1: local_c=4, local_a=5, local_b=4. These local copies are discarded on return.
terms.connects: After f1: main's a=4, b=5, c=6 — unchanged.

terms.meaning: f2 receives addresses &b and &c; through *a and *b it directly reads and writes main's b and c.
terms.example: *a = *b means main's b = main's c (=6); *b = c means main's c = saved old b (=5).
terms.connects: After f2: main's b=6, c=5. a is unchanged at 4.

given.plan: Track variables step by step: f1 has no effect (pass by value), f2 actually swaps b and c (pass by pointer). Then compute c - a - b using final values.

steps.title: Initial state and effect of f1(a, b)
steps.apply:
Initial values: a=4, b=5, c=6.
f1(a, b) passes copies of a and b. Inside f1:
  local c = a (copy) = 4
  local a = b (copy) = 5
  local b = c (saved) = 4
These local variables are discarded when f1 returns.
Result: main's a=4, b=5, c=6 — UNCHANGED.
steps.note: Pass-by-value: f1 cannot modify the caller's variables. The swap happens only to local copies.

steps.title: Effect of f2(&b, &c)
steps.apply:
f2 receives pointer to main's b (value 5) and pointer to main's c (value 6).
Inside f2 (parameter names *a and *b):
  local c = *a = 5  (save main's b)
  *a = *b  =>  main's b = main's c = 6
  *b = c   =>  main's c = 5 (the saved old b)
Result: main's b=6, main's c=5. a remains 4.
steps.note: Pass-by-pointer: dereferencing *a and *b directly modifies the caller's variables b and c.

steps.title: Compute and print c - a - b
steps.apply:
Final values: a=4, b=6, c=5.
c - a - b = 5 - 4 - 6
         = (5 - 4) - 6   (left-to-right associativity)
         = 1 - 6
         = -5
printf("%d", -5) prints: -5

questions.formula_note: Pass-by-value vs pass-by-pointer in C: pass-by-value copies the argument so the caller's variable is unaffected; pass-by-pointer allows the function to modify caller's variables by dereferencing the address.

questions.to_find: The output printed by the program: value of main's local x after x += f1()+f2()+f3()+f2().

understand.plain: There is a global variable x=10. In main, a local x=1 is declared, which shadows the global x inside main. The functions f1 and f2 have no local x, so they see and use the global x=10. f3 declares its own local x=1, so it uses x=1 internally. None of the functions modify the global x. The expression f1()+f2()+f3()+f2() is evaluated: 11+11+2+11=35. Then main's local x (=1) is incremented by 35 to give 36.

keywords.explain: In C, a local variable inside a function shadows a global variable of the same name within that function's scope. Outside that function, the global variable is still unchanged.
keywords.example: In f3: int x=1 shadows global x=10 only inside f3. Global x is still 10 everywhere else.

keywords.explain: A global variable (declared outside functions) is accessible by all functions. A local variable (declared inside a function) is only accessible within that function and shadows any global with the same name.
keywords.example: f1 and f2 see global x=10 since they have no local x; f3 sees its own local x=1.

x='57' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>returns 10+1=11</text><rect x='115' y='55' width='95' height='60' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='162' y='74' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>f2() #1</text><text x='162' y='91' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>global x=10</text><text x='162' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>returns 10+1=11</text><rect x='220' y='55' width='95' height='60' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='267' y='74' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>f3()</text><text x='267' y='91' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>local x=1</text><text x='267' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>returns 1+1=2</text><rect x='325' y='55' width='105' height='60' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='377' y='74' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>f2() #2</text><text x='377' y='91' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>global x=10</text><text x='377' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>returns 10+1=11</text><rect x='10' y='130' width='415' height='30' class='ok' rx='4'/><text x='217' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' style='fill:#fff'>Sum = 11 + 11 + 2 + 11 = 35</text><rect x='10' y='172' width='415' height='35' class='ok' rx='4'/><text x='217' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' style='fill:#fff'>main local x = 1 + 35 = 36  (printf prints 36)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Four function calls: f1 and both f2 calls use global x=10 and each return 11; f3 has local x=1 and returns 2; sum=35; main local x=1+35=36 is printed.

given.aim: Find the value printed by main after evaluating x += f1()+f2()+f3()+f2() with global x=10 and main's local x=1.

terms.meaning: File-scope variable visible to f1 and f2 (which have no local x of their own).
terms.example: f1() accesses global x=10 and returns 10+1=11.
terms.connects: f1 and both calls to f2 each return 11 using this global value. None of them modify it.

terms.meaning: main declares its own local x=1, which shadows the global x inside main only.
terms.example: The printf in main uses main's local x, not global x=10.
terms.connects: x += 35 adds 35 to main's local x=1, giving 36.

terms.meaning: f3 declares its own local x=1, which shadows the global x=10 inside f3.
terms.connects: f3 contributes 2 to the sum, not 11 like f1/f2.

given.plan: Determine which x each function sees (global x=10 for f1 and f2; local x=1 for f3). Evaluate all four function calls and sum their return values. Add the sum to main's local x=1.

steps.title: Determine x visible to each function
steps.apply:
- Global x = 10 (file scope, initialized at program start).
- f1: no local x declared -> sees global x=10. Returns x+1 = 11.
- f2: no local x declared -> sees global x=10. Returns x+1 = 11.
- f3: declares local int x=1 -> shadows global x. Returns x+1 = 1+1 = 2.
- main: declares local int x=1 -> shadows global x inside main.
  None of the functions modify the global x, so it stays 10 throughout.
steps.note: The key insight: f3's local x shadows the global x only inside f3. The global x=10 remains unchanged for f1 and f2.

steps.title: Evaluate f1() + f2() + f3() + f2()
steps.apply:
f1()  = global x + 1 = 10 + 1 = 11
f2()  = global x + 1 = 10 + 1 = 11  (first call)
f3()  = local x + 1  =  1 + 1 =  2  (f3's own local x=1)
f2()  = global x + 1 = 10 + 1 = 11  (second call)

Sum = 11 + 11 + 2 + 11 = 35
steps.note: Since no function modifies global x, both calls to f2 and the call to f1 all return 11.

steps.title: Apply += to main's local x
steps.apply:
main's local x = 1 initially.
x += 35  =>  x = 1 + 35 = 36.
printf("%d", x) prints 36.
steps.note: main's local x (not global x=10) is the target of +=. The result 36 is printed.

questions.formula_note: Variable scoping in C: functions see the global variable unless a local variable of the same name is declared, which shadows the global within that function's scope. No function modifies the global x, so all calls use x=10.
